# Alert

Displays an important status or message that needs the user's attention.

## Contents

- [Installation](#installation)
- [Usage](#usage)
- [Variants and meaning](#variants-and-meaning)
- [Status versus actions](#status-versus-actions)
- [Accessibility and live updates](#accessibility-and-live-updates)

## Installation

Add the Alert component from the configured StackTrace registry.

```shell
npx shadcn-vue@latest add @stacktrace/alert
```

## Usage

Use an alert for a message that belongs in the current page and should remain visible until the surrounding state changes. Compose `AlertTitle` and `AlertDescription` inside `Alert`; an optional icon may reinforce the message, but the text must communicate it without color or imagery.

**Alert example**

```vue
<template>
  <Alert class="max-w-xl">
    <RocketIcon aria-hidden="true" class="size-4" />
    <AlertTitle>Heads up!</AlertTitle>
    <AlertDescription>
      Your project is ready for its first deployment.
    </AlertDescription>
  </Alert>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import {
  Alert,
  AlertDescription,
  AlertTitle,
} from '@/Components/Alert'
import { RocketIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
</script>
```

## Variants and meaning

Choose a variant from the meaning of the message. The default variant is neutral, `positive` confirms a successful or healthy state, and `destructive` reports an error or dangerous condition. These variants describe status; they do not turn the alert into an action or confirmation control.

### Positive

Use a positive alert for a success that should remain associated with the page, such as a completed setup step. Avoid showing routine successes permanently when they do not help the user continue.

**Positive example**

```vue
<template>
  <Alert variant="positive" class="max-w-xl">
    <ShieldCheckIcon aria-hidden="true" class="size-4" />
    <AlertTitle>Protection active</AlertTitle>
    <AlertDescription>
      Your connection is secured with an encrypted tunnel.
    </AlertDescription>
  </Alert>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import {
  Alert,
  AlertDescription,
  AlertTitle,
} from '@/Components/Alert'
import { ShieldCheckIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
</script>
```

### Destructive

Use a destructive alert for an error that blocks progress or requires attention. State what happened and, when possible, how to recover. Do not use destructive styling for an action that has not happened yet.

**Destructive example**

```vue
<template>
  <Alert variant="destructive" class="max-w-xl">
    <TriangleAlertIcon aria-hidden="true" class="size-4" />
    <AlertTitle>Error</AlertTitle>
    <AlertDescription>
      Your session has expired. Please sign in again.
    </AlertDescription>
  </Alert>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import {
  Alert,
  AlertDescription,
  AlertTitle,
} from '@/Components/Alert'
import { TriangleAlertIcon } from '@lucide/vue'
</script>
```

## Status versus actions

An alert communicates a condition; a button performs an action. If the message has a recovery action, place a clearly labeled Button after the description and keep the message understandable without activating it. Use an Alert Dialog or Confirmation Dialog when the user must make a decision before work can continue.

> **Use Sonner for transient confirmation: [Sonner](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/sonner)**
>
> Use a toast for brief, non-critical feedback that does not need a permanent position in the page. Keep validation errors, blocking failures, and instructions near the content they affect.

## Accessibility and live updates

The local `Alert` always renders with `role="alert"`. Assistive technology may announce it when it is inserted or its content changes, so reserve dynamically rendered alerts for important, time-sensitive messages. Do not repeatedly replace the text while a task is progressing; use a stable status label or Progress instead.

Give the alert a concise title and a specific description. Decorative icons should be hidden from assistive technology, and meaning such as success or error must also appear in the text. Move focus only when the user must immediately interact with content elsewhere; an alert itself should not steal focus.

Hosted documentation: [Alert](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/alert)
